risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We piloted the final draft QPL in 23 patients seeing one of three PC doctors from three services in Sydney" and "Patients had about 20 min to review the QPL" and "Before widespread adoption of the intervention a larger scale evaluation, with the inclusion of a control group, would be appropriate." Comment: The paper describes a single-arm pilot in which all enrolled patients received the intervention (QPL); it explicitly notes that a future study should include a control group. Therefore, there was no random allocation sequence for this study.

Summary: The report describes a pilot study in which 23 patients received the question prompt list, but it does not describe any random allocation to groups or any method used to conceal upcoming assignments from those enrolling participants. Comment: Because there is no explicit concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomization or sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes), allocation concealment cannot be confirmed from the article.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients had about 20 min to review the QPL. Clinicians were asked to endorse and refer to the QPL during the consultation." Comment: This indicates both participants and personnel were aware that the intervention (the question prompt list) was being delivered; blinding was not possible or not done. The study outcomes were largely subjective and plausibly influenced by this knowledge, including patient anxiety and patient/doctor feedback on the usefulness of the QPL (e.g. whether it made it easier to ask questions). Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "Prior to the consultation, patients completed a questionnaire regarding their information and involvement (decisional control) preferences, and anxiety levels." and "After the consultation, patients also completed a questionnaire measuring anxiety and responses to the QPL using standard items." and "After 3 weeks, patients completed a further questionnaire regarding the usefulness of the QPL during subsequent contacts with the PC team where that had occurred." Comment: The outcomes included patient self-reported anxiety and perceived usefulness/helpfulness of the QPL, which are subjective outcomes. The patient was therefore the outcome assessor for these measures. The study was a pilot of a QPL intervention with no indication that participants were blinded; patients necessarily knew they received and reviewed the booklet ("Patients had about 20 min to review the QPL"). Because unblinded participants self-assessed subjective outcomes, knowledge of the intervention could have influenced outcome measurement. Under the worst-outcome rule, this makes the domain High risk.

Quote: "Of the 23 participants, 20 completed their post consultation questionnaires, the remaining three became acutely unwell but gave verbal feedback to the research nurse following the consultation. In all, 11 patients completed the 3-week follow-up questionnaires (five patients were too unwell, four died, two did not respond to a reminder and one patient's carer requested no further involvement)." Comment: Post-randomization/missing outcome data were explicitly reported. Immediate post-consultation questionnaire completion was 20/23 (13% missing), but by 3 weeks only 11/23 remained for questionnaire outcomes, i.e. 12/23 missing (52%). This exceeds the threshold for substantial attrition, and reasons (too unwell, death) are plausibly related to the outcomes in this palliative-care setting. No robust missing-data handling is reported. Therefore incomplete outcome data could materially bias the outcome estimates, especially for 3-week patient-reported outcomes.
